planting.note: Give this tall selection a back-of-border spot planted in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun at the original depth and stake if the site is windy. Purple-tinged stems carry golden-yellow flowers with a warm red-orange halo around the center. Deadhead through the season and cut stems to the ground after frost.

planting.note: Plant this compact form in spring toward the front of a sunny bed.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun and average, well-drained ground. This tidy, mounded selection blooms heavily with golden-orange daisies over deep green foliage. Its compact habit rarely needs staking, and light deadheading keeps it flowering into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border for reliable summer color.

directions: Plant with the crown even with the soil and water in well. Slender ray petals give the golden-yellow flowers an airy, semi-double look on upright stems. Deadhead to prolong the display and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the double flowers can be enjoyed.

directions: Set plants at soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot. Also sold as Golden Plume, this old selection bears fully double, pom-pom golden-yellow flowers over a long season. Deadhead spent blooms to keep fresh buds coming through summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun for big single blooms.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surface and keep evenly watered while establishing. Large golden-yellow single flowers sit above dark green foliage on strong stems. Remove faded flowers through the season and cut the plant back after frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; this double form benefits from support.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in full sun and rich, well-drained soil. This selection produces dense, fully double golden-yellow flowers that resemble small dahlias. Deadhead often and consider staking, as the heavy blooms can weigh down the stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest stem and foliage color.

directions: Plant at the original depth in a sunny, well-drained bed. Deep purple-bronze stems and dark foliage contrast sharply with golden-yellow flowers centered in mahogany-red. Deadhead through summer to keep the color show going into fall.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf, variegated form in spring near the front of a bed.

directions: Set plants level with the soil in full sun for the best leaf coloring. This compact selection has cream-and-green variegated foliage topped by golden-yellow daisies. Its short, sturdy habit suits containers and border edges, and light deadheading extends bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun where the variegated leaves show well.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained location. Green-and-cream variegated foliage pairs with golden-orange flowers touched with red toward the center. Deadhead through the season to encourage more blooms.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for its long-lasting double flowers.

directions: Set the crown even with the soil surface in full sun and average soil. This selection carries semi-double to double golden-yellow flowers over a long midsummer season. Deadhead regularly and cut back the clump after frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in a well-drained border.

directions: Plant at the original depth in full sun and give the tall stems space. Also known as Ballet Dancer, it bears semi-double golden-yellow flowers with a quilled, frilly look. Deadhead through summer to keep the flowering going.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny border for its large single blooms.

directions: Set plants level with the soil in full sun and well-drained ground. This robust selection produces very large single golden-yellow flowers on strong upright stems. Remove spent blooms to extend the season and cut back in late fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun in average garden soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and water well to establish. This selection, whose name means sun-shield, offers broad golden-yellow single flowers over a long summer season. Deadhead often and divide the clump every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for bright, semi-double gold flowers.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a sunny, well-drained bed. This selection bears rich golden-yellow semi-double flowers on tall, sturdy stems. Deadhead through the season to keep new buds forming into early fall.

planting.note: Autumn planting lets clumps establish for late-winter flowers.

directions: Site in part shade with rich, moisture-retentive soil and plant so the crown sits level with the ground. Cut away tattered old leaves in late winter to show off the emerging flower stems. Nodding cups in cream, pink, or plum appear from late winter into early spring and often self-sow nearby.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring while soil is cool and moist.

directions: Give these garden hybrids a shaded, well-drained spot enriched with compost, keeping the crown at soil level. Remove old foliage before bloom and top-dress with leaf mold each autumn. Seedlings vary widely in color, so buy plants in flower if you want a specific shade; blooms carry from late winter through mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool woodland soil.

directions: Plant in cool, humus-rich shade where the soil stays evenly moist through spring. This modest deciduous species opens flat, clear green flowers in early spring before dying back for summer. Mulch with leaf litter and leave it undisturbed to bulk up slowly over the years.

planting.note: Plant after hard frost passes; it is the least cold-hardy hellebore.

directions: Grow in a warm, sheltered spot with excellent drainage, or in a pot that can move under cover in cold regions. The marbled silver-veined leaves and soft pink-green flowers make it prized but tender. Keep on the dry side and protect from hard freezes, which it will not survive in colder zones.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained site.

directions: This cross of the Corsican and Majorcan species brings silvery, marbled foliage flushed with pink stems. Plant in gritty, free-draining soil in a sheltered position, as it inherits some tenderness. Clusters of green cups tinted rose appear in late winter and spring; remove old stems once flowering finishes.

planting.note: This sterile hybrid is propagated by division, not seed.

directions: A vigorous cross between the Christmas rose and Corsican hellebore, this sterile plant channels its energy into masses of large white-to-green flowers. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade and divide established clumps to make more. Bloom is long and generous from late winter into spring, and the outward-facing cups show off well.

planting.note: Propagate this hybrid by division in early fall or spring.

directions: This three-way hybrid offers marbled foliage and blush-tinted flowers that age to rose. Set in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade with the crown at grade. Blooms open in winter and hold for weeks; tidy old leaves before flowering to display the color.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions in fall; the hybrid rarely comes true from seed.

directions: A cross of the Christmas rose and Majorcan hellebore, this plant carries handsome veined leaves and pink-flushed flowers on dark stems. Grow in well-drained, compost-enriched shade and keep evenly moist while establishing. The outward-facing blooms open through winter and are a parent of several popular named selections.

planting.note: Very cold-hardy; plant in fall in cool woodland conditions.

directions: One of the earliest and hardiest species, it opens dusky purple-violet cups that often appear before the leaves. Plant in rich, moisture-retentive shade and mulch with leaf mold. It goes dormant in summer, so mark its spot to avoid disturbing the roots.

planting.note: Plant in fall in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Prized for its finely dissected, almost lacy leaves, this species opens small green flowers in early spring. Give it a sharply drained, sunny-to-part-shade spot on the drier side. It is slow but long-lived once settled into lean, gritty ground.

planting.note: Plant in fall in fertile woodland soil.

directions: Named for its lightly scented blooms, this vigorous species carries large yellow-green flowers in early spring. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained shade and keep moist during active growth. It is one of the more robust green-flowered species and settles in quickly.

planting.note: Plant in fall; tolerates summer dormancy well.

directions: This Balkan species opens bright yellow-green, faintly fragrant cups in early spring above fresh green leaves. Grow in rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. It dies back in summer, so pair it with later perennials to fill the space.

planting.note: Plant in fall in cool, humus-rich shade.

directions: An early bloomer whose purple-red flowers often open before the leaves unfurl. Plant in fertile, well-drained woodland soil and leave undisturbed to build up. The new foliage may be flushed purple, adding to its early-season appeal.

planting.note: Plant in fall; a small, delicate woodland species.

directions: One of the smallest hellebores, it bears dainty green flowers on slender stems in early spring. Site in cool, moist shade with rich soil and let it self-sow gently. Its delicate scale suits the front of a woodland planting.

planting.note: Plant in fall in fertile, well-drained shade.

directions: A small early species with hairy stems and purple-flushed green flowers that open before the foliage. Plant in humus-rich woodland soil in part shade. Leave the summer-dormant crown undisturbed and top-dress with leaf mold each autumn.

planting.note: Plant in fall; valued by collectors for flower color range.

directions: This variable species can produce flowers in shades from green to deep purple, and has contributed dusky tones to garden hybrids. Grow in fertile, well-drained shade and let it die back over summer. Mark the crown so you do not dig into it during dormancy.

planting.note: A named selection best grown from divisions to keep its large flowers.

directions: This classic selection is known for unusually large, rounded pure white flowers with a green eye. Plant in rich, well-drained shade with the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist. Blooms open in winter; divide clumps in early autumn to preserve the strain.

planting.note: An early-flowering form; plant divisions in fall.

directions: Selected for its very early bloom, this Christmas rose can open white flowers in late autumn or early winter. Grow in fertile, well-drained part shade with the crown at grade. Protect the earliest flowers from mud splash with a light gravel mulch.

planting.note: A Gold Collection selection sold as nursery plants.

directions: A compact, free-flowering Christmas rose that opens broad white blooms aging to pink. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich shade and keep the crown level with the soil. It flowers heavily in winter and works well in containers near an entryway.

planting.note: A vigorous Gold Collection Christmas rose from divisions.

directions: One of the earliest and largest-flowered Christmas roses, it bears big white cups on sturdy stems. Grow in rich, well-drained shade and keep moist while establishing. Expect a long winter display; remove faded leaves to spotlight the blooms.

planting.note: A popular Gold Collection hybrid grown from divisions.

directions: Named for its red stems and pink-flushed flowers that deepen as they age, set over blue-green marbled leaves. Plant in well-drained, compost-rich shade with the crown at soil level. The outward-facing blooms carry from late winter into spring on a compact, tidy plant.

planting.note: A Gold Collection hybrid sold as potted plants.

directions: Opens creamy white flowers washed with cinnamon and rose on dark stems, flowering very early. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained shade and keep the crown level with the ground. It blooms across winter and holds its color for a long stretch.

planting.note: A robust Gold Collection hybrid grown from divisions.

directions: A strong grower with large flowers that open white and age through pink to rose-green. Plant in well-drained, fertile shade with the crown at grade and mulch with leaf mold. The big outward-facing blooms make a bold late-winter show.

planting.note: A Gold Collection selection grown from divisions.

directions: Bears rosy flowers on red stems that deepen to burgundy-rose as they age, over silver-veined leaves. Plant in rich, well-drained shade with the crown level. Its long-lasting winter blooms and colorful stems make it a favorite for containers and borders.

planting.note: A widely sold hybrid propagated by division.

directions: A compact, sturdy hybrid with ivory buds flushed rose and green that open to creamy outward-facing flowers. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich part shade with the crown at soil level. It blooms heavily in late winter and keeps neat blue-green foliage year round.

planting.note: A Rodney Davey Marbled selection grown from tissue-culture divisions.

directions: Prized for deep red-veined marbled leaves and rich wine-red outward-facing flowers. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade with the crown at grade and keep evenly moist. The marbled foliage looks good all season, and blooms carry through late winter into spring.

planting.note: A Frostkiss marbled-leaf selection from divisions.

directions: Carries pink flowers on dark stems above heavily silver-marbled foliage. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained part shade with the crown level with the soil. The outward-facing blooms and patterned leaves give a long season of interest from winter into spring.

planting.note: A Frostkiss marbled selection grown from divisions.

directions: Opens clean white outward-facing flowers over silver-marbled green leaves. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade and keep the crown at soil level. Remove any tired old leaves before bloom to show off the flowers, which last well through late winter.

planting.note: A Frostkiss marbled-foliage selection from divisions.

directions: Bears bright pink-red outward-facing flowers above boldly marbled leaves. Plant in well-drained, compost-enriched part shade with the crown at grade. The showy blooms face outward for easy viewing and carry through the late-winter season.

planting.note: A Winter Jewels double, best bought in flower and grown from divisions.

directions: A double-flowered selection with dark slate-black petals that glow deep maroon in sunlight. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade with the crown level and mulch with leaf mold. The layered blooms open in late winter and last for weeks; strip old leaves to display them.

planting.note: A Winter Jewels double strain; buy in bloom for color.

directions: Produces double golden-yellow flowers, many with red picotee edges or speckling. Plant in rich, well-drained part shade with the crown at soil level. The fully double blooms are long-lasting in late winter; remove tattered foliage before they open.

planting.note: A Winter Jewels double selection; seed strains vary.

directions: Opens richly saturated wine-red double flowers on a vigorous plant. Grow in fertile, well-drained shade with the crown level and keep moist while establishing. The deep color reads best against a light mulch; blooms carry through the late-winter weeks.

planting.note: A Double Ellen strain selection; buy in flower for the doubles.

directions: Carries fully double purple flowers with neatly layered petals on strong stems. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained part shade with the crown at grade. Trim away old leaves before the blooms open in late winter so the doubles stand out.

planting.note: A Lady Series seed strain; individual plants vary somewhat.

directions: This seed strain produces large flowers in deep slate-purple to near-blue tones. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade with the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist. Sow fresh seed in outdoor pots for the warm-then-cold cycle it needs; blooms appear in late winter once plants mature.

planting.note: A Lady Series seed strain in white shades.

directions: Bears large white flowers, some lightly speckled, on a robust seed-grown plant. Grow in rich, well-drained part shade with the crown level and mulch with leaf mold. Remove old foliage before bloom; flowers open across late winter into spring.

planting.note: A Lady Series seed strain in deep red tones.

directions: Produces dusky red to deep rose flowers on a vigorous, easy plant. Plant in well-drained, compost-rich shade with the crown at grade. Cut back tired leaves in late winter and the nodding cups will show clearly as they open into spring.

planting.note: A Lady Series seed strain in soft yellow shades.

directions: Opens clear yellow flowers, some with fine red speckling near the center. Plant in fertile, well-drained part shade with the crown level with the soil. Give it steady moisture while young; blooms brighten the winter garden and mature plants flower reliably.

planting.note: A silver-foliage Corsican selection grown from divisions.

directions: Selected for boldly silvered, spiny blue-green leaves on a large upright clump. Plant in well-drained soil in part shade to a fair amount of sun, and give it room to spread. Clusters of green cups top the stems in late winter; cut old flowering stems to the base afterward.

planting.note: A red-stemmed form that self-sows; grow from fresh seed or plants.

directions: Distinguished by reddish stems and leaf stalks that set off the finely divided dark foliage. Plant in sharply drained part shade and let a few seed heads ripen to keep it going. The green red-rimmed bells open over a long winter-to-spring season on branching stems.

planting.note: A pink-stemmed Stern's selection; plant in spring in a warm spot.

directions: Valued for pink-suffused stems and silvery marbled leaves with rose-tinted green flowers. Plant in gritty, free-draining soil in a sheltered, warmer position, as it is less cold-hardy. Trim spent stems after the late-winter to spring bloom to keep the clump shapely.

planting.note: A sterile Gold Collection hybrid grown from divisions.

directions: A robust sterile hybrid that pours out large green-white flowers over a long season. Plant in fertile, well-drained shade with the crown level and keep moist while establishing. Because it sets no seed, it flowers heavily for months; divide clumps to increase your stock.

planting.note: A marbled-leaf hybrid grown from divisions.

directions: Grown as much for its silver-veined, cream-marbled foliage as for its cream flowers aging to pink. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich part shade with the crown at grade. The patterned leaves give year-round interest while blooms open in winter.

planting.note: A Winter Jewels double green strain; buy in bloom for color.

directions: Opens double green flowers finely veined and edged with maroon. Plant in fertile, well-drained part shade with the crown at soil level and mulch with leaf mold. Strip away old leaves before the long-lasting late-winter blooms appear so they show clearly.

planting.note: A Winter Jewels double white strain; buy in flower for the doubles.

directions: Bears fully double white flowers, some with a faint green cast or light speckling. Plant in humus-rich, well-drained shade with the crown level and keep moist while establishing. Remove tattered foliage before bloom so the crisp doubles stand out in late winter.

planting.note: A Frostkiss marbled selection grown from divisions.

directions: Opens soft pink outward-facing flowers that deepen with age over silver-marbled leaves. Plant in fertile, well-drained part shade with the crown at grade. The marbled foliage carries interest all year while blooms last through late winter into spring.

planting.note: Sow in spring for bloom the following year, or in early summer to establish rosettes before winter.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and press in lightly, as light aids germination; keep the soil evenly moist until seedlings appear. Grow on as a leafy rosette the first season and expect tall, fragrant lilac flower spikes in late spring of the second year. Cut back after flowering to limit heavy self-seeding, which can become weedy.

planting.note: Sow in spring or early summer; plants flower strongly in their second year.

directions: Press seed lightly into a firm bed and keep moist until germination, which is quick in mild soil. The pure-white form carries the same clove-scented evening perfume as the lilac type and glows in twilight borders. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage a lighter second flush and to keep self-sown seedlings in check.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions in spring, since this sterile double sets no seed.

directions: Because the fully double flowers are sterile, propagate only by basal cuttings or careful division taken in spring or early autumn. Plant in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and avoid letting the crown dry out through summer. Expect long-lasting, densely packed lilac rosette-flowers on tall spikes in early summer; shear after bloom to keep the clump vigorous.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring; the double white is sterile and cannot be grown from seed.

directions: Increase this old cottage-garden double from spring cuttings or divisions, as it produces no viable seed. Give it rich, cool, reliably moist soil and light afternoon shade in hot regions to prolong the display. The tiered spikes of double white florets are strongly scented in the evening; trim spent stems to sustain the plant over several years.

planting.note: Establish from spring-rooted cuttings or divisions; this double form is seed-sterile.

directions: Propagate the rich purple double only by vegetative means, taking short basal shoots in spring for rooting. Plant into humus-rich, moisture-retentive ground and keep watered during dry spells to prevent premature decline. Deeply saturated double spikes open in early summer with the family's characteristic sweet evening fragrance.

planting.note: Sow in spring or summer; the compact white form flowers in its second season.

directions: Sow lightly on the surface and keep moist, thinning seedlings to give each rosette room to develop. This shorter, pure-white selection suits the front of a border better than the tall species and remains fragrant at dusk. Cut spent stems promptly to prolong the plant and reduce volunteer seedlings.

planting.note: Sow in spring or early summer for flowering the following year on lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow on gritty, free-draining soil and press in lightly, keeping the bed just moist until seedlings emerge. This night-scented species carries muted cream to purple-veined flowers that look faded by day but release a powerful perfume after dark. Give it sun and sharp drainage, and let a few pods ripen to renew the short-lived plants.

planting.note: Sow spring or summer; flowers in the second year and self-sows in suitable sites.

directions: Surface-sow and firm lightly, keeping evenly moist until germination. This eastern relative of common dame's rocket bears fragrant lilac-purple spikes and grows much like the familiar species. Deadhead after bloom to manage self-seeding and to coax fresh basal growth.

planting.note: Sow in spring or late summer; very cold-hardy and flowers in its second season.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep the seedbed moist through germination, which can be uneven in cool soil. This hardy species tolerates severe winters and produces fragrant lilac to purple flower spikes in early summer. Allow a few seedpods to mature so the short-lived clumps renew themselves.

planting.note: Sow in spring on warm, well-drained soil; a Mediterranean species best in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Sow into gritty, sharply drained soil and keep barely moist until seedlings establish. This southern-European species has deeply lobed grey-green foliage and dusky cream-to-violet flowers that are strongly scented at night. Give it full sun and lean soil, protecting the crown from winter wet, and let seed ripen to maintain the planting.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out once frost danger passes.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface of a moist starting mix and keep it bright and humid until sprouts appear. Grow in well-drained soil and divide clumps every three or four years to keep them vigorous. Slender stems carry bright coral-red bells above the leaves from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and keep it evenly moist under bright light. Plant in loose, well-drained soil where it gets morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates. Clouds of minute white flowers float on wiry stems above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted plants in spring or early fall.

directions: Set plants with the crown at soil level in rich, well-drained ground and water in well. This villosa-derived hybrid tolerates heat and humidity; give it some afternoon shade to keep the warm apricot leaf color bright. Amber to caramel foliage is topped by delicate cream flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown just at soil level and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Partial shade preserves the deep near-black leaf color, though it takes more sun in cool climates. Glossy black-purple leaves form a tidy mound with airy white flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant in rich, well-drained soil with the crown at grade and avoid burying the growing point. Partial shade keeps the plum coloring vivid in hot regions. Metallic plum-purple leaves with darker veining are topped by slim cream flower stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; shade keeps color bright.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in moist, well-drained soil and protect from harsh afternoon sun that can scorch the bright leaves. New growth emerges chartreuse and matures to lime green. Small white flowers appear on short stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at grade in humus-rich soil and keep evenly moist through the first season. This villosa hybrid shrugs off heat and humidity; the ruffled leaves shift from amber to umber with rosy undersides. Airy sprays of tiny flowers rise in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground and water regularly while establishing. Large peach-colored leaves overlaid with a silver sheen hold up in heat and humidity. Wispy white flower stems appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; give shade in hot climates.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, moisture-retentive soil and mulch to keep roots cool. This heat-loving villosa selection glows bright chartreuse-yellow in partial shade. Tiny cream flowers rise on airy stems in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level and keep steadily moist so the leaves color well. Dark purple-black foliage is speckled with hot-pink spots that fade to cream as the season warms. Slim white flower stalks appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; light shade keeps color vivid.

directions: Set plants at grade in well-drained soil and give bright dappled light for the most intense leaf color. The ruffled foliage glows chartreuse-green all season. Airy white flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and mulch to hold moisture. This villosa-type hybrid takes heat well and shows bright red new growth that deepens to burgundy in summer heat. Cream flowers appear on short stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in fertile soil and keep evenly watered as it establishes. Large rounded leaves shift from rose-pink in spring to deeper purple-rose in summer. Tall airy flower stems carry small white blooms in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in well-drained ground and water in thoroughly. The glossy purple leaves hold their color through the season and the plant keeps a dense, vigorous habit. Purple flower stems bearing small blooms rise above the mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; tolerant of varied light.

directions: Set plants at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. Silvery leaves are veined and edged in deep purple-red, a vigorous americana-type selection with good garden durability. Airy sprays of tiny cream flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in humus-rich soil and give room to spread, as this is a large villosa hybrid. Big leaves emerge peach-amber and mature to cinnamon, holding well in heat and humidity. Cream flowers rise on airy stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil and keep steadily moist. The heavily ruffled, glossy near-black leaves form a striking mound that holds color all season. Slim flower stems bearing small pale blooms rise in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; blooms repeatedly through summer.

directions: Plant at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground and deadhead spent stems to encourage more bloom. Silvery-green leaves form a compact mound beneath long-lasting rosy-red flowers. Expect repeated flushes of bloom from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; grown mainly for flowers.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil and remove faded stems to prolong flowering. This sanguinea-type selection is prized for its fragrant, bright vermilion-red bells. Flowering stems rise well above the green foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist. The intensely ruffled leaves emerge fiery red and deepen to burgundy-purple through the season. Small white flowers appear on slender stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; color shifts with the seasons.

directions: Set plants at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to protect the crown in winter. Leaves glow peachy-orange in the growing season and turn deep plum-red in cool weather. Airy cream flowers rise on wiry stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; flowers and foliage both valued.

directions: Plant at soil level in well-drained ground and deadhead to keep flowers coming. This heat-tolerant villosa hybrid pairs bronze-red foliage with red-orange bell flowers. Blooms appear from early summer and can repeat into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in fertile soil and keep it evenly moist while rooting in. The silvery plum-purple leaves have a metallic sheen and rosy undersides. Small pale flowers rise on thin stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. The rounded leaves are burnished silver over dark purple veins, coloring best in partial shade. Airy white flowers rise on slim stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; heat and humidity tolerant.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and give room for a large clump. This vigorous villosa selection shows deep mahogany-brown leaves that hold up in Southern heat. Tall airy stems carry tiny cream flowers in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; blooms late in the season.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in humus-rich soil and give it space, as it forms a large green mound. Grown for its bloom as much as its fuzzy apple-green leaves, it tolerates heat and shade well. Tall spikes of creamy-white flowers open in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at grade in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. The deeply ruffled leaves are silver-purple with contrasting darker veins and rosy undersides. Slim flower stems carry small pale blooms in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in rich, well-drained ground and mulch to conserve moisture. The frosted violet-purple leaves deepen in color through the growing season. Airy pink-tinged flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; part shade keeps color bright.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil and provide dappled light for the brightest foliage. The heavily ruffled leaves glow bright lime-chartreuse all season on this heat-tolerant villosa hybrid. Cream flowers rise on airy stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in fertile, well-drained soil and water in well. Chartreuse-gold leaves are overlaid with red veining that deepens as the season progresses. Small cream flowers appear on slender stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; heat and humidity tolerant.

directions: Set the crown at grade in humus-rich soil and keep evenly moist. This villosa hybrid shows warm gold-green leaves with a red-brown center that changes tone through the season. Airy cream flowers rise on wiry stems in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in well-drained ground and mulch to keep roots cool. The silvery ginger-tan leaves shift toward apricot as temperatures cool. Small pale flowers rise on thin stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; part shade keeps color best.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and provide dappled light. The bright yellow leaves take on a lemony glow and resist fading in part shade. Airy cream flowers rise on slim stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and keep steadily moist. The large glossy leaves are among the darkest, holding a deep black-purple through the season. Slim flower stems carry small pale blooms in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at grade in fertile, well-drained soil and water in well. New leaves emerge bright orange and mature toward amber-green with the season. Small cream flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in humus-rich soil and mulch to conserve moisture. The rounded leaves emerge rose-pink and mature to a deep silvery rose-purple. Airy pale flowers rise on slim stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; part of the heat-tolerant Carnival series.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist as it establishes. Part of a compact, heat-tolerant series, its leaves glow warm peach-amber with rosy undertones. Small cream flowers rise on short stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. This heat-tolerant villosa hybrid shows silvery purple leaves with a grape-toned overlay. Airy flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter; grown mainly for flowers.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed under bright light and transplant seedlings after frost. This classic garden hybrid group is grown for tall wands of pink to red bell flowers over green foliage. Bloom rises well above the leaves from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in humus-rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. The leaves are washed in pewter-silver over dark purple veins, coloring best in partial shade. Airy white flowers rise on slim stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level and keep evenly moist. The tightly curled and ruffled leaves emerge crimson-red and deepen to burgundy through the season. Small cream flowers rise on slender stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; bred for cold hardiness.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil and mulch for winter protection. This villosa-based hybrid was bred for cold hardiness and strong roots, with red foliage that holds through the season. Cream flowers rise on airy stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant at soil level in fertile, well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. The leaves blend amber, coral, and lime tones with rosy undersides that shift through the season. Small cream flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer.

planting.note: Set out plants or divisions in spring or early fall in cool, shaded ground.

directions: Plant in humus-rich woodland soil with the crown set level with the surface and mulch with leaf litter. This running species spreads by surface stolons to form a groundcover, so give it room and keep the soil evenly moist. Frothy spires of tiny white to pale-pink flowers rise above the maple-shaped leaves in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant in cool, moist shade in spring; native to Pacific Northwest forests.

directions: Choose a consistently damp, shaded spot and set the crown flush with the soil. The three-part leaves and airy sprays of tiny white bells suit stream banks and shaded rain gardens. Delicate flower stems appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant in shade with even moisture in spring or fall.

directions: Site in rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep the crown at grade. This clumping species from East Asia carries slender wands of small white-to-pink flowers above rounded lobed foliage. Bloom comes in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in humus-rich shade.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in cool, organic ground and mulch lightly. Deeply cut leaves marked with a central dark blotch form a neat clump topped by dense pink-flushed flower spikes. Shear spent stems to encourage a repeat flush after the heavy spring bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist, shaded soil.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in loose woodland soil and keep it evenly damp. Glossy, deeply dissected leaves with bold red-black centers set off large clusters of pink-budded white flowers. Trim faded spikes to tidy the mound and prompt fresh bloom.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in dappled shade.

directions: Plant in cool, humus-rich soil with the crown flush to the surface. It sends up unusually tall, tightly packed rosy-pink flower spires above heavily cut, dark-veined foliage. Deadhead the spent wands to extend the display through spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in shaded, organic soil.

directions: Site in rich, moisture-holding ground and keep the crown at soil level. Sharply lacerated leaves with dark central markings spread on short runners to make a loose mat. Airy white flower spikes rise in mid to late spring; trim old growth to freshen the clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a spreading shade groundcover.

directions: Set plants a foot or so apart and let the surface stolons knit into a carpet in cool shade. Green leaves veined with a dark central stripe blush bronze-red in cold weather for winter interest. Wispy white flower spikes appear over the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant clumps in spring in rich, shaded soil.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in cool, organic soil and mulch to retain moisture. Deeply divided leaves carry a striking dark central pattern and stay in tidy non-running clumps. Slim pink-tinged flower spikes rise well above the foliage across a long spring season.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in shaded woodland soil.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in humus-rich shade and keep evenly moist. Broad lobed leaves show a bold black center that reddens in fall and winter. White flower plumes appear in spring; shear old foliage as the fresh leaves emerge.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dappled shade.

directions: Plant in rich, moisture-retentive soil with the crown at grade. This clumping selection is prized for full, rounded spikes of clear pink flowers over green lobed leaves. Remove spent stems to keep the bloom coming through late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low spreading groundcover.

directions: Set plants close and let the runners spread into a low, dense mat in shade. Small dark-marked leaves hug the ground while short white flower spikes stand just above. Its compact vigorous habit makes it an excellent shady groundcover.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in rich shaded soil.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the soil and allow it to spread by stolons into a groundcover. Heart-shaped green leaves take on deep burgundy fall and winter tones along the veins. Foamy white-to-pink flower spikes rise over the mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, humus-rich ground.

directions: Set the crown at grade in cool, rich soil and keep evenly moist. Bright chartreuse leaves are slashed by dramatic near-black centers for season-long foliage contrast. White flower spikes appear in spring over the tidy non-running clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in dappled woodland shade.

directions: Plant in loose, organic soil with the crown level and mulch to hold moisture. Finely cut, dark-centered leaves form a compact clump beneath a generous show of soft pink flowers. Deadhead to prolong the spring display.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in shaded, rich soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in cool humus and keep steadily moist. Named for its oak-shaped lobed leaves, it stays in a neat clump and offers reliable pink-tinted bloom. Shear spent spikes to encourage tidy fresh foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing shade groundcover.

directions: Set plants a foot apart and let the long stolons run to cover shady ground. Low, dark-marked leaves trail readily, making it useful cascading over walls or in containers. Short white flower spikes appear over the mat in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, moist shade.

directions: Plant the crown flush with the soil in humus-rich ground and keep evenly damp. Glossy emerald leaves with dark markings form a dense clump under abundant pale flower spikes. Remove faded stems to keep the plant looking fresh.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shaded, organic-rich soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in cool, moist humus and mulch lightly. Deeply and finely cut snowflake-shaped leaves are heavily overlaid with near-black centers. Soft white flower spikes rise above the striking foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, well-drained, partly shaded soil.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in humus-rich, well-drained ground and avoid waterlogging. This sterile Heuchera-Tiarella hybrid shows gold leaves with bright red veining that shifts to orange and rose through the seasons. Wispy white flower spikes appear in late spring; divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist but well-drained shade.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Large maple-shaped leaves glow cinnamon-orange with a darker center, deepening in cooler weather. Short sprays of white flowers rise in late spring above the broad mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in partly shaded, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the crown level in rich, well-drained ground and keep evenly moist without sogginess. Vivid yellow-gold leaves carry a bold red central blotch for standout foliage in the shade garden. White flower spikes appear in late spring; some afternoon shade preserves the leaf color.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained shade.

directions: Plant with the crown at soil level in humus-rich, well-drained earth. Bright golden leaves marked with red veins form a broad low mound and hold color well in part shade. Airy white flowers rise on short stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. Rounded chartreuse-to-caramel leaves feature a dramatic deep maroon center, giving a two-tone eclipse effect. Short white flower spikes appear in late spring over the vigorous mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing selection for baskets or slopes.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in well-drained soil, or set in a container to show off the cascading stems. Trailing branches carry caramel-to-rose leaves with darker centers that shift color through the year. Small white flowers appear in late spring; the spreading habit suits hanging baskets and wall edges.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Set the crown level in rich, well-drained soil and give bright dappled light for best color. Electric gold, deeply cut leaves are streaked with vivid red centers in a zebra-like pattern. White flower spikes rise in late spring above the compact mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained shade.

directions: Plant with the crown at grade in humus-rich, well-drained soil. Blue-green lobed leaves carry a deep purple center, and the plant blooms unusually heavily for its type. Rosy-pink flower spikes appear in late spring and often rebloom if deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, partly shaded soil.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, well-drained ground and mulch to hold moisture. Rounded lobed leaves turn warm caramel-amber tones that deepen to red-caramel in fall. Short white flower sprays appear in late spring above the mounded foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing type for containers or banks.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in well-drained soil or place in a basket to display the cascading stems. Golden-yellow leaves with bold red veins trail as the season progresses. Small white flowers appear in late spring; excellent spilling over walls and container edges.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing selection in part shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil or grow in a container to feature the cascading habit. Trailing stems bear yellow-green leaves marked with red centers that trail attractively. White flowers appear in late spring; ideal for hanging baskets and slope plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained shade.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. Amber-gold leaves carry a striking dark central star that stands out in shade. White flower spikes appear in late spring above the tidy mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade in humus-rich, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. Deeply cut, elongated leaves are silver-green with dark purple veining for a refined look. Creamy white flower spikes rise in late spring over the arching foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing type for baskets or edges.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in well-drained soil or use in a container to show the cascading branches. Deeply cut copper-orange leaves trail and hold warm tones through much of the season. Small white flowers appear in late spring; well suited to hanging baskets and low walls.

planting.note: Plant in spring in rich, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Set the crown level in well-drained, humus-rich soil and mulch to retain moisture. Deeply divided near-black leaves give strong dark contrast against lighter shade plantings. White flower spikes rise in late spring above the glossy dark mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained shade.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. Silvery leaves are traced with a network of dark purple veins that suggests cracked ice. Airy white flowers appear in late spring above the low mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Set the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch lightly. Green leaves with subtle darker centers form the backdrop for an unusually showy floral display. Bright pink flower spikes rise in late spring and often rebloom when deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, partly shaded soil.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained ground and keep evenly moist. Bronze-purple leaves are overlaid with a shimmering silver sheen for cool foliage contrast. Pink-budded white flower spikes rise in late spring above the mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a trailing type for containers or slopes.

directions: Set the crown at grade in well-drained soil or plant in a basket to feature the trailing stems. Deeply lobed plum-purple leaves cascade and hold rich color in part shade. Small white flowers appear in late spring; a strong choice for hanging baskets and wall edges.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist, well-drained partial shade.

directions: Plant the crown at grade in rich, well-drained soil and mulch to conserve moisture. Green lobed leaves with darker centers support a heavy show of soft pink flowers. The tall flower spikes appear in late spring and rebloom when spent stems are removed.

planting.note: Set container plants out in spring after the danger of hard frost has passed.

directions: Plant at the same depth it grew in the pot in rich, moisture-retentive ground. Feed in spring and never let the roots dry out to keep the foliage lush. Look for brilliant ruby-red saucers up to nine inches wide from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Transplant into warm spring soil once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Site in full sun with steady moisture and space plants for good air movement. Cut the woody stems down each spring and mark the spot, as new growth appears very late. Soft pink flowers carrying a crimson center eye cover the plant through late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so the copper foliage colors up in strong sun.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a sunny, moist bed and mulch to hold water. The coppery-purple maple-shaped leaves color best with full sun and even feeding. Enormous pale-pink flowers with a red eye and deep-pink veins open across late summer.

planting.note: Move plants outdoors in spring once the soil is workable and frost has passed.

directions: Give this vigorous grower room, rich soil, and constant moisture at the root zone. Dark green red-tinged foliage sets off the flowers, and a spring cutback keeps the clump tidy. Expect huge deep-red blooms nearly a foot across from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, reliably damp location.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the soil consistently moist for the best flower size. Cut stems back hard in spring and be patient with the late emergence. Pure white flowers with no eye, up to ten inches across, appear through late summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost so purple-toned foliage develops in full sun.

directions: Plant in a moist sunny bed and water generously through the heat of summer. The deeply lobed foliage carries a purple cast that deepens with sun exposure. Lavender-purple flowers with darker veining open from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost into full sun and damp soil.

directions: Plant level with the surrounding soil and keep the bed evenly moist all season. This one wants full sun and steady feeding to reach its full size. Creamy pale-yellow to near-white flowers with a red throat open across late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes and soil has warmed.

directions: Choose a sunny, moist spot and keep the roots from drying during summer heat. Cut the stems back each spring and allow for the plant's late start. Large clear-pink flowers with overlapping petals cover the bushy clump in late summer.

planting.note: Transplant into warm spring soil after the last hard frost.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with consistently moist soil. Its shorter habit suits the front of a border, but it still needs generous water. Deep velvety-red flowers appear from midsummer through early fall.

common_name: Turn of the Century Hardy Hibiscus

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost into a bright, damp bed.

directions: Give this tall grower plenty of space, full sun, and abundant moisture. Support may help in windy sites since stems reach seven feet. Distinctive pinwheel flowers blend red and pink stripes across each petal in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun for the fullest bloom.

directions: Plant this rounded selection in rich, moist soil and full sun. Its bushy habit needs no staking, but steady water keeps the show going. Ruffled lavender-pink flowers eight inches wide open from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Move outdoors in spring after frost into a sunny, moist location.

directions: Set in full sun with consistent moisture and cut stems to the ground in spring. The dense mounded form flowers heavily without pinching. Deep cranberry-red blooms with a slight sheen appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so the dark foliage colors in full sun.

directions: Site in full sun to bring out the near-black leaf color and keep soil moist. This mounded plant makes a striking contrast even before it blooms. Bright scarlet-red flowers glow against the dark foliage from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost into a bright, moisture-rich bed.

directions: Plant this compact, wide grower in full sun with even moisture. Its low mounded habit suits smaller beds and needs no support. Pale-pink flowers with a bold red eye sit above dark leaves from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost into full sun and damp soil.

directions: Give this large selection room, full sun, and plenty of water. The purple-tinged maple-like leaves add interest through the season. Huge pink flowers with darker veins and a red center open in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun with steady moisture.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the soil evenly moist for continuous flowering. Its bushy rounded form holds up without staking. White-and-pink pinwheel flowers with a red eye sit above chocolate-toned foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost so dark leaves develop in sun.

directions: Give this large plant full sun and abundant moisture at the roots. The near-black foliage makes a bold backdrop all season long. Deep red flowers up to nine inches wide open from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a sunny, consistently moist bed.

directions: Set in full sun to intensify the dark foliage and keep soil moist. The compact mound flowers freely from top to bottom. Bright magenta-pink flowers with a small dark eye appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Move outdoors in spring after frost into full sun and moist soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with rich, moisture-holding soil for the best flower count. This wide grower makes a full rounded mound without pinching. Soft creamy-yellow flowers aging to buttery cream cover the plant in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in full sun with reliable moisture.

directions: Give this compact plant full sun and steady water throughout summer. Its tidy rounded shape works well toward the middle of a border. Ruffled medium-pink flowers with a red eye open from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost into a bright, damp spot.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep evenly moist for the longest bloom period. The rounded habit holds its shape with no support. Large white-to-blush flowers with a red eye open from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost into full sun and moist ground.

directions: Plant this low, wide selection in full sun with consistent moisture. Its compact spreading form flowers all along the stems. Pink-and-white pinwheel flowers with a red eye open from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so red-tinged leaves color in the sun.

directions: Give this upright grower full sun and generous moisture through summer. The dark red-green foliage sets off the flowers beautifully. Vivid true-red blooms with narrow petals open from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost into full sun with moist soil.

directions: Plant in full sun to darken the foliage and keep the roots moist. The rounded mound flowers freely without any pinching. Soft pink flowers with a deeper eye rise over near-black leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom on this dwarf type.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm soil and grow on in bright light before transplanting. This dwarf, well-branched plant fits containers and small beds with steady water. Deep-red flowers up to eight inches wide bloom the first summer from an early sowing.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter to bloom the first season.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow a quarter inch deep in warm, moist mix. Keep this compact plant well watered in full sun for tidy repeat bloom. Pink-and-white swirled flowers open across the first and every following summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for bloom the first year.

directions: Start seed warm and shallow, then move seedlings to full sun once established. This dwarf plant needs constant moisture but no staking. Large pure-white flowers with a red center eye open through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter to flower the first summer.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and grow seedlings under strong light. Set the dwarf plants in full sun and never let them dry out. Rich rose-pink flowers up to eight inches wide bloom all summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for a first-year show.

directions: Soak and sow the seed warm, then grow on before setting out after frost. This compact selection stays neat with even moisture and full sun. Pale blush-pink flowers deepening toward the center open through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost so bronze foliage colors in full sun.

directions: Set this dwarf, well-branched plant in full sun with steady moisture. The dark bronze-green leaves stay attractive even between flushes. Pink flowers with a darker eye densely cover the compact mound all summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after frost into full sun and moist soil.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun and keep the soil consistently damp. Its dense branching means bloom from the base to the tips. Soft blush-pink flowers with a red eye open across the summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter to bloom the first summer.

directions: Nick and soak the seed, then sow warm and shallow for quick germination. This dwarf seed strain flowers the first year and suits pots or small beds. Large overlapping-petal flowers in red, pink, or white open through summer.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors for tall first-year plants.

directions: Soak the seed overnight, then sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil. This tall seed strain blooms the first year if started early and kept moist. Dinner-plate flowers up to ten inches across come in red, rose, pink, and white.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for bloom the first season.

directions: Nick, soak, and sow the seed warm to speed up germination. Grow this seed strain in full sun with abundant water for the largest flowers. Huge saucer blooms in a mix of red, pink, and white open across summer.

planting.note: Set divisions out after the soil warms in spring, keeping the crown level with the ground.

directions: Plant the crown at soil level in rich, moisture-retentive ground and water deeply during dry spells. This species tolerates more sun than most hostas but rewards a little afternoon shade. Watch for large, powerfully fragrant white trumpets on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring as the pointed shoots emerge.

directions: Give this big blue-leaved hosta a shady, humus-rich bed with room to spread. The waxy coating on the foliage fades if it sits in too much direct sun, so favor dappled or full shade. Pale lavender-white flowers rise just above the mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions into cool, shaded, well-worked soil.

directions: Site this heavily corrugated blue selection in full to partial shade with steady moisture. The deeply puckered leaves are slug-resistant thanks to their thick substance. Near-white flowers appear on short scapes among the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Sow seed or set divisions in spring; this species comes true from seed.

directions: Plant in a shaded, evenly moist spot and keep the glossy green heart-shaped leaves free of drought stress. Unlike most hostas this species sets viable seed that breeds true, so seedlings resemble the parent. Deep violet bell-shaped flowers open on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Lift and split clumps in spring, replanting divisions promptly.

directions: Set this white-centered, wavy-margined hosta in partial shade with consistent moisture. The twisted, variegated leaves brighten shady edges and pathways. Lavender flowers rise well above the mound in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring where a dense low groundcover is wanted.

directions: Use this fast-spreading, narrow-leaved hosta to knit together shaded ground and edge beds. Keep it evenly watered and it will form a glossy dark green carpet. Purple flowers appear late, in late summer into early fall.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and replant at the original depth.

directions: This dependable, vigorous hosta grows in most shaded soils and forms a tidy mound of gray-green leaves. Keep the root zone cool and moist through summer. Lavender flowers open on upright scapes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant slow-growing divisions in spring in a sheltered shady spot.

directions: This slow, compact species forms cupped, blue-gray leaves that hold dew like little bowls. Give it deep shade and patience, as it takes several years to bulk up. Near-white flowers appear just above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this early riser appreciates protection from late frosts.

directions: Give this large hosta a shaded bed with rich, moist soil and room to reach full size. It emerges early, so shield the golden-margined leaves from hard spring frosts. Pale lavender flowers rise above the mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into cool, richly amended soil.

directions: This classic pairs puckered blue-green centers with wide chartreuse-gold margins. Keep it in partial to full shade, since strong sun can scorch the leaf edges. Near-white flowers rise on short scapes in early summer.

planting.note: Plant spring divisions where filtered light will bring out the gold.

directions: The leaves open green and mature to bright gold with a thin dark green edge, coloring best with morning sun. Too much shade keeps the centers greener, too much sun bleaches them. Lavender flowers appear on upright scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded, moisture-retentive bed.

directions: A tough, reliable hosta with olive-green leaves edged in a clean narrow white margin. It takes shade well and is more sun-tolerant than many white-edged types. Lavender flowers open on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in bright shade to feed the cream-centered leaves.

directions: A striking, slow-growing hosta with cream-to-gold centers ringed by blue-green margins. It needs gentle morning light but must be shielded from harsh sun that scorches the pale centers. Near-white flowers rise above the mound in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade to protect the white leaf centers.

directions: A dramatic sport of 'Patriot' with pure white centers and dark green margins. The white tissue burns easily, so keep it in shade with consistent moisture. Lavender flowers appear on upright scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring with morning sun to light up the gold centers.

directions: Bright gold centers glow between dark green margins, and the leaves seem to catch light like a stained-glass panel. Some morning sun deepens the gold without scorching. Large, fragrant near-white flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.
